Methodology Showcase · Bangkok 2018–2023

Measuring Urban Construction under Data Fragmentation

A multi-signal observability framework using OSM building records, VIIRS nighttime lights, Dynamic World built-up probability, satellite validation, and road-network context.

1. Research Logic

Data-fragmented city

Formal building-permit data are incomplete or unavailable. Construction must be inferred from imperfect proxy signals.

→

Three observable signals

Structural records, functional activity, and physical land-cover change are measured separately.

→

Observability framework

Disagreement across signals is treated as evidence, not noise.

2. Three Signals

Structural

OSM Building Records

Measures recorded building area from ohsome API. Captures mapped built form, but may also reflect mapping updates and documentation intensity.

Functional

VIIRS Nighttime Lights

Measures changes in illumination and economic activity. Captures functional activation rather than construction itself.

Physical

Dynamic World Built-up

Measures satellite-derived built-up probability. Captures land-cover transformation and physical expansion.

3. Processing Pipeline

Step 1 · Extract OSM building history

Use ohsome API to obtain OSM building area for 2018 and 2023. Aggregate to 1 km Bangkok grid.

Step 2 · Extract remote-sensing signals through GEE

Compute annual VIIRS nighttime-light intensity and Dynamic World built-up probability for 2018 and 2023.

Step 3 · Build grid-level panel

Merge structural, functional, and physical signals into a common grid-level dataset covering 1,570 Bangkok grid cells.

Step 4 · Quantify signal divergence

Use pairwise correlations and Moran's I to test whether signals are weakly related but spatially structured.

Step 5 · Classify observability types

Define high-signal cells using quantile thresholds and classify grids into four observability types.

Step 6 · Aggregate to district mechanisms

Translate grid-level signal combinations into district-level transformation mechanisms.

Step 7 · Validate and contextualize

Use Sentinel-2 visual validation and OSM road-network context to interpret whether signals reflect mapping, activation, or physical expansion.

4. Result Figures and Interpretation

Figure 1. Conceptual framework. The framework starts from a data-fragmented city and separates urban construction into structural, functional, and physical signals. The key methodological move is to treat disagreement across signals as analytical evidence rather than noise.

Figure 2. OSM building records. OSM building area increases are concentrated in inner-city and already developed districts, supporting the interpretation that OSM growth is partly a structural or documentation signal rather than a direct measure of new construction.

Figure 3. Nighttime lights. VIIRS NTL captures functional activity. Its change pattern differs from OSM building growth, showing that recorded built form and activity intensity are not interchangeable measurements.

Figure 4. Built-up probability. Dynamic World built-up change is more visible in peripheral and transitional areas, helping distinguish land-cover expansion from mapping or documentation updates.

Figure 5. Grid-level observability. Red grids show rare high-confidence overlap between OSM and NTL (3.7% at 80th percentile threshold). Purple and blue grids show single-signal dominance, confirming that construction observability is fragmented.

Figure 6. District-level mechanisms of urban transformation in Bangkok. Inner-city districts (Pathum Wan, Wattana, Ratchathewi, Bang Rak) are classified as structural/mapping update zones. Peripheral districts (Thawi Wattana, Taling Chan, Min Buri, Lat Krabang) show physical expansion or functional-physical combinations.

Appendix Figure A. Satellite-based visual validation. Sentinel-2 true-colour composites (2018 vs 2023) for three representative grids. Pathum Wan shows dense existing urban fabric with no visible physical change (mapping update). Don Muang shows functional activation near the airport corridor. Thawi Wattana shows visible transition from agricultural to built-up land cover (physical expansion). Cases provide illustrative support for the mechanism classification; they are not exhaustive.

6. Observability Types

Grid-level classification

High-signal thresholds identify where structural and functional signals overlap or diverge. The limited overlap between high OSM and high NTL is the central empirical finding.

High OSM + High NTL High OSM only High NTL only Low / mixed

District-level mechanisms

Grid-level signals are aggregated into interpretable transformation mechanisms: structural/mapping update, functional activation, physical expansion, and hybrid types.

Structural / mapping update Functional activation Physical expansion Functional + physical

7. Threshold Sensitivity

Threshold quantileHigh OSM + High NTLHigh OSM onlyHigh NTL onlyTriple high
0.709.6%20.4%20.4%4.3%
0.756.7%18.3%18.3%2.7%
0.803.7%16.3%16.3%1.3%
0.852.2%12.8%12.8%0.7%

Signal fragmentation is not an artifact of the threshold choice. Across all thresholds, overlap remains limited while single-signal dominance remains common.

8. Validation and Context

Case A

Pathum Wan

High OSM growth in an already dense inner-city area. Sentinel-2 comparison shows little visible physical expansion, supporting the mapping/documentation interpretation.

Case B

Don Muang

Functional activation near transport infrastructure. NTL captures activity change not equivalent to building-record growth.

Case C

Thawi Wattana

Physical expansion case. Satellite imagery and built-up probability indicate visible land-cover transformation from agricultural to built-up.


Road-network analysis is used as policy and infrastructure context, not causal evidence. OSM road changes may also reflect mapping updates, reinforcing the need to distinguish physical transformation from documentation processes.

9. Paper Contribution

Methodological novelty

Unlike conventional multi-source urban sensing, this framework does not collapse multiple proxies into a composite index. It treats cross-signal disagreement as the measurement object.

Urban theory implication

Observability is spatially unequal. Inner-city areas may be over-documented, peripheral areas may physically expand before being mapped, and functional activation may appear without formal construction records.

Bangkok Urban Observability Project · Methodology showcase · Target journal: Environment and Planning B
